questions.question: What is the main purpose of the Temporal Docs MCP Server?

choices.text: It stores your Temporal workflow data in the cloud
It gives AI assistants real-time access to Temporal documentation so their answers are based on current docs rather than older training data
It automatically generates Temporal workflows from plain English descriptions
It monitors your running Temporal workflows and alerts you to errors

questions.explanation: The Temporal Docs MCP Server connects Temporal's documentation directly to AI assistants. Without it, an AI assistant can only rely on information it was trained on, which may be outdated. With the MCP server, the AI can look up current Temporal documentation in real time, giving you more accurate and up-to-date answers about Temporal concepts and features.

questions.question: What is the difference between the 'Temporal Developer Skill' and the 'Temporal Cloud Skill'?

choices.text: The Developer Skill is for beginners and the Cloud Skill is for experts
The Developer Skill covers writing Temporal code (like workflows and activities), while the Cloud Skill focuses on connectivity and configuration issues with Temporal Cloud
The Developer Skill works only with Python, while the Cloud Skill works with all languages
They are identical — 'Developer' and 'Cloud' are just two names for the same skill

questions.explanation: These two Skills serve different purposes. The Temporal Developer Skill gives your AI coding agent deep knowledge about how to write Temporal code — things like workflow rules, activity patterns, and error handling. The Temporal Cloud Skill, on the other hand, is focused on helping troubleshoot issues specific to Temporal Cloud, such as connectivity, authentication, and configuration problems. Think of one as a coding guide and the other as a cloud operations guide.

questions.question: According to the Temporal quickstart page, what is the recommended first thing to do when getting started with Temporal in any programming language?

choices.text: Deploy a Temporal Cloud account and configure namespaces
Read all the Temporal documentation before writing any code
Install the SDK for your chosen language and run a Hello World Workflow
Set up a production-ready Worker and connect it to a database

questions.explanation: The Temporal quickstart page keeps things simple for beginners: pick your programming language, install the corresponding Temporal SDK, and run a basic 'Hello World' Workflow. This hands-on approach lets you see Temporal in action right away without needing to understand all the details upfront. Temporal supports many popular languages including Go, Java, Python, TypeScript, PHP, Ruby, and .NET.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Temporal Developer Skill when installed in an AI coding agent like Claude Code or Cursor?

choices.text: It provides a GUI dashboard for monitoring running Temporal workflows directly inside the IDE
It gives the AI agent expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model, including determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, and common gotchas
It automatically generates boilerplate Temporal workflow code based on natural language descriptions
It connects the AI agent to a live Temporal Cloud namespace for real-time workflow execution

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill gives AI coding agents domain-specific expertise about Temporal's programming model — covering workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, worker configuration, versioning, and common gotchas. It is not a code generator or a live connection to Temporal Cloud; it provides the agent with knowledge so it can give accurate guidance when you are writing Temporal code.

questions.question: What distinguishes the Temporal Docs MCP Server from the Temporal Developer Skill?

choices.text: The MCP Server is only available for Claude Desktop, while the Skill works with any agent
The Skill requires a paid Temporal Cloud account, while the MCP Server is free
The MCP Server gives AI tools real-time access to current Temporal documentation, whereas the Skill provides pre-packaged domain knowledge baked into the agent
The MCP Server replaces the Skill and should always be used instead of it

questions.explanation: The Temporal Docs MCP Server connects AI assistants to live, up-to-date Temporal documentation so responses draw from current docs rather than the model's training data. The Temporal Developer Skill, by contrast, is a packaged set of domain expertise installed into the agent. They serve complementary purposes: the Skill provides deep pattern knowledge while the MCP Server ensures answers reflect the latest documentation.

questions.question: When adding the Temporal Docs MCP Server to Claude Code, what is the difference between running the command with '--scope user' versus without it?

choices.text: '--scope user' installs the server globally for all projects, while omitting it stores the configuration only in the current project's .mcp.json file
'--scope user' requires Google authentication, while omitting it allows anonymous access
'--scope user' enables read-write access to documentation, while omitting it provides read-only access
There is no functional difference; '--scope user' is just an alias for the default behavior

questions.explanation: Running 'claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai' registers the MCP server globally so it is available across all your projects. Omitting '--scope user' stores the configuration in the project's local .mcp.json file, making the server available only within that specific project. This is a useful distinction when you want to share the MCP configuration with your team via source control versus keeping it personal.

questions.question: Which installation method for the Temporal Developer Skill is explicitly stated to work with Claude Code, Codex, Cline, and other agents (not just one specific tool)?

choices.text: The Claude Code Plugin method using /plugin marketplace add
The Cursor Marketplace method using /add-plugin temporal
The npx method using 'npx skills add https://github.com/temporalio/skill-temporal-developer'
The Manual method using git clone into ~/.claude/skills/

questions.explanation: The npx tab in the documentation is explicitly labeled as working with Claude Code, Codex, Cline, and other agents. The Claude Code Plugin method is specific to Claude Code, the Cursor method is specific to Cursor, and the Manual git clone method defaults to the ~/.claude/skills/ directory (a Claude-specific path), though it notes you can change the target directory for other agents. The npx approach is the most broadly compatible option.

questions.question: Your team is using an AI coding assistant to help write Temporal workflows. The assistant generates code that calls `time.Sleep()` directly inside a workflow function, reasoning that it's simpler than using `workflow.Sleep()`. The AI was trained on general Go code and doesn't have Temporal-specific context. Which tool would most directly address the root cause of this problem?

choices.text: Switch to a different AI assistant that has a larger training dataset with more Go examples.
Install the Temporal Developer Skill, which gives the AI expert-level knowledge of workflow determinism rules and common gotchas like avoiding non-deterministic primitives.
Add the Temporal Docs MCP Server so the AI can look up the workflow.Sleep() API signature in real-time documentation.
Write a custom linter that detects time.Sleep() calls in workflow code and blocks the AI from generating such patterns.

questions.explanation: The root cause is that the AI lacks domain-specific knowledge of Temporal's programming model — specifically, that workflow code must be deterministic and cannot use native concurrency/timing primitives. The Temporal Developer Skill is explicitly designed to give AI agents 'expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model — workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, worker configuration, versioning, and common gotchas.' This is different from the MCP Server (option C), which provides real-time documentation access but doesn't embed deep conceptual expertise about WHY certain patterns are required. A linter (option D) is a reactive workaround, not a root cause fix. The underlying principle: AI tools without Temporal-specific training will apply general programming patterns that violate Temporal's determinism requirements, producing subtly broken workflows that may only fail during replay.

questions.question: A developer configures the Temporal Docs MCP Server for their Claude Desktop setup and notices that answers about Temporal Cloud authentication are much more accurate than before. A colleague argues they should just use the Temporal Developer Skill instead because 'Skills and MCP do the same thing.' How should you explain the distinction?

choices.text: The colleague is correct — both tools provide the same capability, and choosing between them is purely a matter of personal preference and workflow.
Skills work offline while the MCP Server requires internet access, so the choice depends on your network environment.

Skills embed domain-specific expertise (conceptual knowledge, patterns, gotchas) into the agent, while the MCP Server provides real-time access to current documentation — they serve complementary purposes and can be used together.
The MCP Server is strictly superior because it always has the latest information, making Skills obsolete once the MCP Server is configured.

questions.explanation: These two tools solve different problems. The Temporal Developer Skill gives AI agents 'expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model — workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, worker configuration, versioning, and common gotchas.' This is baked-in conceptual understanding. The MCP Server, by contrast, gives AI tools 'real-time access to documentation, so responses draw from current docs rather than training data' — it's about freshness and accuracy of reference material. A skilled engineer knows both the principles of their domain AND where to look up current specifications. Similarly, Skills + MCP together give an AI agent both deep Temporal expertise and up-to-date reference information. Option D is wrong because the MCP Server provides documentation lookup, not conceptual reasoning about why Temporal's programming model works the way it does.

questions.question: Your organization uses Temporal Cloud and engineers frequently struggle with connectivity and authentication configuration issues when onboarding. You want AI assistants to help diagnose these problems accurately. Which Temporal AI tooling should you prioritize, and why?

choices.text: Install the Temporal Developer Skill, because it covers all Temporal topics including Cloud connectivity and authentication.
Configure the Temporal Docs MCP Server, because real-time documentation access is always sufficient for diagnosing connectivity issues.
Install the Temporal Cloud Skill specifically, which is designed to help AI agents troubleshoot Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues.
Neither — AI assistants cannot reliably help with Temporal Cloud authentication issues because these are environment-specific problems that require human intervention.

questions.explanation: Temporal provides two distinct Skills for different problem domains. The Temporal Developer Skill covers the programming model (workflow determinism, activity patterns, retry policies, etc.), while the Temporal Cloud Skill is specifically designed to 'help your AI coding agent troubleshoot Temporal Cloud connectivity, authentication, and configuration issues.' This separation reflects a real architectural distinction: writing correct Temporal code and configuring Temporal Cloud infrastructure are different skill sets. Option A is a common mistake — assuming one tool covers everything. The Developer Skill does NOT claim to cover Cloud-specific operational concerns. This matters in production because Cloud authentication failures (mTLS certificate issues, namespace configuration, API key problems) require specialized knowledge that is separate from workflow programming patterns.

questions.question: A team wants to add the Temporal Docs MCP Server to Claude Code for a sensitive project, but they are concerned about privacy because the server 'requires anonymous authentication using any Google account.' They worry Temporal can see which engineers are accessing documentation. Should this block adoption?

choices.text: Yes — any authentication mechanism means the provider can collect user identity information, so this is a legitimate privacy concern that requires legal review before adoption.

No — the authentication is explicitly anonymous. Temporal's documentation states the Google account authentication is used solely to enforce rate limits and prevent abuse, and that Temporal cannot see nor collect any contact information.
No — MCP servers never transmit any metadata, so authentication is purely local and no information reaches Temporal's servers.
Yes — Google account authentication means Google collects the data, creating a third-party data sharing concern regardless of what Temporal sees.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly addresses this concern: 'The server requires anonymous authentication using any Google account to enforce rate limits and prevent abuse. We cannot see nor do we collect any contact information from this.' Understanding the actual purpose of authentication matters for making accurate risk assessments. The authentication here serves a technical function (rate limiting, abuse prevention) rather than identity tracking. Option C is wrong because it incorrectly claims no metadata reaches the server — the authentication does interact with the server, but without collecting contact information. Option A and D represent overcautious interpretations that contradict the explicit documentation. In production environments, accurately reading privacy disclosures rather than assuming the worst prevents unnecessary blockers to adopting useful tooling.

choices.text: The StartToCloseTimeout of 5 minutes is too short for document processing; the AI should have set ScheduleToCloseTimeout instead, and the MCP server would have returned outdated timeout documentation.

Calling time.Now() inside a workflow function violates Temporal's determinism requirement. On workflow replay, time.Now() will return a different value, causing the idempotency key to differ across executions — a non-determinism error. The Temporal Developer Skill provides domain knowledge but cannot statically analyze generated code for runtime determinism violations before the engineer reviews it.
The RetryPolicy MaximumAttempts of 3 is insufficient; with an AI workload, activities should have unlimited retries. The MCP server's real-time docs would show the correct default retry behavior.

The workflow will fail because ProcessDocument activity must be registered with a separate worker configuration when using AI-generated code, and the MCP server cannot verify worker registration at code-generation time.

questions.explanation:
This question targets one of the most dangerous classes of Temporal bugs: non-determinism violations introduced by seemingly innocent code. Temporal's execution model works by replaying workflow history to reconstruct state after a worker crash or restart. During replay, the workflow function executes again, but activities are NOT re-executed — their results are pulled from history. This means every line of workflow code that isn't an activity or side effect must produce identical results on every execution, for the entire lifetime of the workflow.

time.Now() is wall-clock time. On the first execution (say, Tuesday at 2pm), idempotencyKey = 1700000000. On replay (say, after a worker restart on Wednesday), idempotencyKey = 1700086400. Temporal's determinism checker detects that the workflow is making different decisions across executions and panics with a non-determinism error, permanently blocking that workflow execution.

The correct fix is to use workflow.Now(ctx), which returns the timestamp recorded in workflow history — the same value on every replay.

Now for the deeper insight about AI tooling: The Temporal Developer Skill gives an AI agent conceptual knowledge about determinism rules, retry policies, and common gotchas. The MCP Server gives the agent real-time access to current documentation. Neither of these tools performs static analysis of the generated code against Temporal's runtime determinism constraints. The AI may 'know' that non-determinism is bad but still generate time.Now() because it's pattern-matching from general Go programming knowledge. This is why AI-assisted Temporal development still requires human review by engineers who understand the determinism model deeply.

The production lesson: treat any Go standard library call that produces non-idempotent output (time.Now(), rand.Int(), uuid.New(), os.Getenv() for mutable values) as a red flag inside workflow functions. Temporal provides workflow.Now(), workflow.GetLogger(), workflow.SideEffect(), and workflow.MutableSideEffect() as safe alternatives. No AI tool — regardless of how current its documentation access is — replaces understanding this architectural constraint.

questions.question: A platform team is standardizing how engineers get Temporal expertise. They configure Claude Code with both the Temporal Developer Skill AND the Temporal Docs MCP Server. An engineer asks the agent to help debug why their Python workflow occasionally gets stuck after Continue-As-New. The agent, drawing on both the Skill's embedded knowledge and real-time MCP documentation, suggests the workflow history is being reset correctly. However, the engineer notices the workflow only gets stuck when a Signal arrives within approximately 100ms of the Continue-As-New call. Given this timing constraint, what is the most likely root cause, and what does this reveal about the limits of AI-assisted Temporal debugging?

choices.text: The MCP server has a rate limit that causes documentation lookups to fail under high concurrency, so the agent cannot retrieve the signal handling documentation needed to diagnose the issue.

Signals sent during the Continue-As-New transition can be lost if they arrive after the old execution closes but before the new execution's first task is processed, since Continue-As-New starts a fresh execution with empty history. The agent's knowledge of this race condition may be correct in theory, but it cannot observe your actual Temporal cluster's event timing, worker poll intervals, or whether your signal sending code uses the old vs. new workflow ID run — gaps no documentation lookup can bridge.
Python workflows cannot use Continue-As-New with signals because the GIL prevents concurrent signal processing; this is a known SDK limitation the Temporal Developer Skill should have caught.

The Temporal Developer Skill and MCP Server conflict when both are active, causing the agent to receive contradictory information about signal delivery guarantees, which leads to incorrect debugging advice.

questions.explanation:
This question exposes a subtle but devastating production race condition involving Continue-As-New and Signals, and uses it to teach an important lesson about AI tooling boundaries.

The Continue-As-New mechanism closes the current workflow execution and atomically starts a new execution with the same Workflow ID but a new Run ID, carrying over only the data you explicitly pass to it. The key architectural detail: Signals are delivered to a specific Run ID, or to the 'latest' run if sent by Workflow ID only. There is a window — typically milliseconds but potentially longer under load — between when the old execution completes its Continue-As-New command and when the new execution's first workflow task is scheduled and picked up by a worker.

If a Signal arrives during this window and your signal sender used the old Run ID (or if there's a routing edge case), the Signal can either: (1) be rejected as delivered to a closed execution, (2) be buffered and delivered to the new execution, or (3) in buggy client code, be silently dropped. The ~100ms timing hint in the question is a strong indicator of a worker poll interval issue — the new execution isn't being picked up fast enough, and signals sent during that gap exhibit inconsistent behavior.

The correct mitigation is to use workflow.GetSignalChannel() in the new execution to drain any signals that may have been buffered, ensure signal senders use Workflow ID without Run ID for forward compatibility, and consider using Update-with-Start patterns (newer Temporal feature) for operations that must be atomic with workflow creation.

Now the deeper lesson about AI tooling: The Temporal Developer Skill provides embedded conceptual expertise. The MCP Server provides current documentation. Together they give an AI agent excellent theoretical knowledge. But they cannot: (1) observe your cluster's actual event log timing, (2) read your specific signal-sending client code to check whether it's using Run ID, (3) measure your worker's actual poll interval under your current load, or (4) correlate your workflow stuck events with server-side metrics. Real production debugging requires combining AI-assisted conceptual reasoning with direct inspection of Temporal's Web UI event history, tctl/temporal CLI queries, and application metrics. The engineers who get the most value from AI Temporal tooling are those who use it to quickly retrieve conceptual frameworks, then apply those frameworks to empirical observations the AI cannot make.
